---
title: "The Drug Discovery Process"
subtitle: "Unit 5, Part 5 — BIOL09044 Inflammation & Cancer"
author: "Dr Mark Paul-Clark"
institute: "Nottingham Trent University"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../styles-slides.scss]
    slide-number: true
    progress: true
    transition: fade
    chalkboard: true
    footer: "Unit 5: Cortisol | NTU"
---

## Drug Discovery: Context

NCX-1015 provides us with a real-world case study to understand the drug discovery pipeline.

Where does NCX-1015 sit?

- ✅ Target identification (GR / inflammation)
- ✅ Target validation (known GC pharmacology)
- ✅ Lead identification & optimisation (10 compounds → NCX-1015)
- ✅ Preclinical testing (in vitro + in vivo models)
- ❓ Clinical candidate selection
- ❓ Clinical trials

---

## The Drug Discovery Cycle

### Core Iterative Loop

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 9
flowchart TD
    A[Collection of<br/>Compounds] --> B[Primary Assays<br/>In Vitro]
    B --> C[SAR &<br/>Lead Selection]
    C --> D[Chemical<br/>Synthesis]
    D --> B
    B --> E[Secondary Assays<br/>Metabolism In Vivo]
    C --> F[Characterisation<br/>Ligand Complex]
    F --> D
    E --> G[Clinical<br/>Candidate]
    
    style A fill:#2980b9,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2980b9,color:#fff
    style C fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff
    style D fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff
    style E fill:#7f8c8d,color:#fff
    style G fill:#f39c12,color:#000
```

**SAR** = Structure-Activity Relationship

The cycle is iterative — compounds are continuously refined based on pharmacological and metabolic data.

---

## Traditional Drug Discovery Timeline

| Stage | Duration | Cumulative |
|-------|----------|-----------|
| Target Identification | 0.5–1 year | ~1 year |
| Target Validation | 0.5–1 year | ~2 years |
| Lead Identification | 0.5–1 year | ~3 years |
| Candidate Optimisation | 2–3 years | ~5–6 years |
| Pre-Clinical Studies | 1–2 years | ~7–8 years |
| Clinical Trials | 4–6 years | **~12–14 years** |

::: {.callout-important}
The traditional pipeline from target to market takes **12–14 years** on average.
:::

---

## Drug Reprofiling: A Shortcut

### SWITCH Drug Re-Profiling Approach

Instead of starting from scratch:

1. Begin at **Lead Identification** stage with an existing known compound
2. Use **data mining** of existing pharmacological, safety, and clinical data
3. Skip target identification, validation, and much of preclinical work
4. Move directly to **Clinical Proof of Concept** trials

**Timeline:** 0.5–1 year (data mining) → Clinical trial

::: {.callout-tip}
NCX-1015 is essentially a **reprofiling strategy** — starting with a known drug (prednisolone) and chemically modifying it to improve the therapeutic profile while leveraging existing knowledge of GC pharmacology.
:::

---

## The Attrition Funnel

### From 10,000 Compounds to 1 Approved Drug

| Step | Stage | Purpose |
|------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Discovery & Screening | High-throughput screening, target validation |
| 2 | Lead Optimisation | Combinatorial chemistry, structure-based design |
| 3 | ADMET | Absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion, toxicity |
| 4 | Clinical Trials | Phases I–III |
| 5 | NDA Approved | Regulatory submission |

**10,000–20,000** candidate compounds enter the pipeline.

**1** drug reaches the market.

::: {.callout-warning}
The success rate is approximately **0.005–0.01%** — making drug discovery one of the highest-risk, highest-investment endeavours in science.
:::

---

## Financial Burden {.smaller}

### Capitalised Costs by Stage (US$ millions)

| Stage | Cost | Duration | Attrition (%) | Programs |
|-------|------|----------|---------------|----------|
| Target validation & lead | $674M | 4.5 years | 49% | 24.3 |
| Pre-clinical (lead) | — | 1.0 year | 31% | 12.4 |
| Phase I | $273M | 1.5 years | 36% | 8.6 |
| Phase II | $319M | 2.5 years | 56% | 4.6 |
| Phase III | $314M | 2.5 years | 30% | 1.6 |
| Submission | $48M | 1.5 years | 9% | 1.1 → **1** |

### The "Valley of Death"
The transition from pre-clinical to Phase I is called the **translation gap** or "valley of death" — where many promising preclinical findings fail to translate to human efficacy.

---

## Why Do So Many Products Fail?

### Key Reasons for Attrition

- **Lack of efficacy** in humans (despite preclinical success)
- **Toxicity** not predicted by animal models
- **Poor pharmacokinetics** (ADMET failures)
- **Commercial viability** reassessment
- **Manufacturing** challenges at scale

### The Academic–Industry Interface

Drug discovery increasingly relies on collaboration between:

- **Academic researchers** (target identification, biological mechanism, animal models)
- **Academic drug discovery institutes** (HTS, medicinal chemistry, lead optimisation)
- **Industry partners** (ADMET, scale-up, clinical trials)
- Supported by federal funding, foundations, and philanthropic groups

---

## NCX-1015: Where in the Pipeline?

Mapping our case study to the drug discovery stages:

| Stage | NCX-1015 Status |
|-------|----------------|
| Target identification | ✅ GR / inflammation pathway |
| Target validation | ✅ Extensive GC literature |
| Lead identification | ✅ 10 compounds screened (SAR) |
| Lead optimisation | ✅ NCX-1015 selected |
| Preclinical — in vitro | ✅ GR binding, NO release, CD163, IL-1β |
| Preclinical — in vivo (acute) | ✅ Peritonitis, intravital microscopy |
| Preclinical — in vivo (chronic) | ✅ Arthritis, air pouch |
| Bone safety | ✅ Osteoclast assays, pyridinoline |
| Oral bioavailability | ✅ Demonstrated |
| Clinical candidate | ❓ Further PK/tox studies needed |
| Clinical trials | ❌ Not yet entered |

---

## Summary: Part 5

:::{.incremental}
- The drug discovery pipeline spans **12–14 years** from target to market
- Only **1 in 10,000–20,000** compounds reaches approval
- Total capitalised cost exceeds **$1.6 billion** per approved drug
- The **translation gap** (valley of death) between preclinical and clinical stages is where most programmes fail
- **Drug reprofiling** offers a faster, cheaper route by leveraging existing compound data
- NCX-1015 represents a successful **preclinical drug discovery programme** that addressed a genuine clinical unmet need
- The compound demonstrates how **academic–industry collaboration** drives innovation
:::

---

## Self-Assessment

<details>
<summary>**Q1:** At what stage does the highest probability of attrition occur in the drug discovery pipeline, and why?</summary>
**Phase II clinical trials** have the highest attrition rate at **56%**. This is because Phase II is the first time a compound is tested for **efficacy** in the target patient population. Many compounds that show promise in preclinical models and are safe in Phase I (healthy volunteers) fail to demonstrate sufficient therapeutic benefit in actual patients — the translation gap.
</details>

<details>
<summary>**Q2:** How does NCX-1015 exemplify a drug reprofiling approach?</summary>
NCX-1015 starts with **prednisolone**, a well-established drug with decades of clinical data on efficacy, safety, pharmacokinetics, and side effects. Rather than discovering an entirely new compound, the researchers **chemically modified** an existing drug to improve its therapeutic profile. This leverages existing GC knowledge, bypasses early target identification/validation stages, and accelerates the path to clinical testing.
</details>

<details>
<summary>**Q3:** What additional studies would NCX-1015 need before entering Phase I clinical trials?</summary>
Key studies would include: formal **toxicology** (acute and chronic, in two species), detailed **pharmacokinetics** (absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion), **reproductive toxicology**, **genotoxicity** assessment, **GMP-grade manufacturing** process development, and preparation of an **Investigational New Drug (IND)** application or equivalent regulatory submission.
</details>
